pages.text: as we believe that these systems may increase the storage life. The latter can be attributed to the fact that the agent is activated at a later date i.e. in situ . Another novelty in this research is the use of a healing agent that expands upon polymerization. This expansion acts as a driving force for the healing agent to come out of the cap- sules and bigger crack spaces may be ﬁlled. Additionally, the chal- lenge of using concrete samples instead of mortar was tackled in this research. Several techniques have already been used to quantify the autonomous crack healing efﬁciency in concrete. Mostly, regain of mechanical properties such as strength and stiffness are used to evaluate the healing efﬁciency [6–11]. Depending on the healing mechanism used, strength and stiffness regain of 50 till 150% were noted. Also the inﬂuence of crack healing on transport properties is used as evaluation criterion. This is very important since a decrease in transport properties may prolong the service life of the struc- ture. Nishiwaki et al. [12] found that when a water permeability test was performed onto a self-closing system, the water leakage was very much reduced. Our previous study indicated that the water permeability of a specimen after self-healing was only slightly higher than the water permeability obtained for a crack which was manually healed [11]. Morimoto et al. [13] measured the air permeability of cementitious samples before cracking, immediately after cracking and after crack healing (storing speci- mens under moist conditions). They concluded that the air perme- ability coefﬁcient decreased dramatically with increase of curing age due to hydration of un-hydrated cement particles. Some researchers also made use of non-destructive acoustic techniques such as resonance frequency analysis and ultrasonic measure- ments [14,15] to evaluate the healing efﬁciency. It was noticed that due to autonomous crack healing, the resonance frequency re- turned to its original value and that the transmission time of ultra- sonic waves decreased as the waves could travel through the healing agent in the crack and thus follow the shortest pathway. Another acoustic technique, which may be very useful to eval- uate self-healing and which has been used in the present research is acoustic emission (AE) analysis. In contrast to resonance fre- quency and ultrasound analysis, where the source emitting the waves is applied to the surface of the material, in AE the waves are produced by and emitted within the material [16]. When a material is loaded above its ultimate load capacity, this will result in localized zones of cracking. Thereby, elastic energy is released, which propagates through the structure in the form of elastic waves that can be recorded as transient AE signals. AE has already been used by several researchers in order to study the concrete fracture behavior, using parameter or signal based AE methods [16]. Many AE applications include simply counting of the AE activity recording the total number of events occurring during crack formation. To get more information, signal parameters such as rise time, duration and maximum amplitude are extracted [16]. Ohtsu et al. [17] reported being even able to estimate the current condition of civil structures by performance of a parameter based analysis, but these applications are somehow sophisticated if no AE signals are recorded and parameters are used alone to interpret the results. AE signal-based techniq

Acoustic emission analysis for the quantiﬁcation of autonomous crack healing in concrete Kim Van Tittelboom a, Nele De Belie a,⇑, Frank Lehmann b, Christian U. Grosse c a Magnel Laboratory for Concrete Research, Department of Structural Engineering, Ghent University, Technologiepark Zwijnaarde 904, B-9052 Ghent, Belgium b Material Testing Institute, Department of Non-Destructive Testing and Monitoring Techniques, Stuttgart University, Pfaffenwaldring 4, D-70550 Stuttgart, Germany c Non-Destructive Testing Lab, Center for Building Materials, Technische Universität München, Baumbachstrasse 7, D-81245 Munich, Germany article info Article history: Received 25 January 2011 Received in revised form 23 August 2011 Accepted 29 August 2011 Available online 20 October 2011 Keywords: Self-healing concrete Ceramic tubes Polymers Mechanical properties Non-destructive testing abstract As half of the annual construction budget is spent on remediation of existing structures, self-healing of concrete, which is very sensitive to cracking, would be highly desirable. In this research, encapsulated healing agents were embedded in the concrete matrix in order to obtain self-healing properties. Upon cracking, the capsules break and the healing agent is released, resulting in crack repair. The efﬁciency of this crack healing technique was evaluated by means of mechanical tests and by using acoustic emis- sion analysis. It was shown that due to autonomous crack repair, more than 80% of the original strength and stiffness can be regained. Events with an energy higher than the energy related to concrete cracking indicated breakage of the capsules. Upon reloading of beams with untreated cracks, the released energy was lower compared to beams with healed cracks. From this study it was shown that AE is a suitable technique to evaluate self-healing of cracks in concrete. /C2112011 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. 1. Introduction Concrete is frequently used as construction material because of its favorable properties such as: high strength, low cost, freedom of design and resistance to high temperatures [1]. One adverse prop- erty of concrete is its sensitivity to crack formation. Cracks endan- ger the durability of the structure as aggressive substances may penetrate into the matrix along these cracks and cause damage. Consequently, cracks may become larger and the reinforcement may be exposed to the environment. Once the reinforcement starts to corrode, total collapse of the structure may be feared. Therefore, it seems obvious that repair of damage is absolutely indispensable. However, large costs are coupled to these maintenance works. Be- sides the direct costs due to maintenance and repair, also the indi- rect costs, due to loss in productivity and occurrence of trafﬁc jams, should be taken into account [2]. Accordingly, immediate and autonomous crack repair in con- crete is a property which is strived for. Self-healing of cracks in concrete is actually an old and well known phenomenon. Concrete in itself possesses some autogenous healing properties due to the continuing hydration of clinker minerals and precipitation of CaCO 3 [3]. These mechanisms are some of the reasons why old con- crete structures, like the Roman Pantheon, are still existing. How- ever, these autogenous healing properties are limited to small cracks [4] and are only effective when water is available inside the crack [5]. Furthermore, today much ﬁner cement grains are used so less unreacted

a period of 24 h. After demolding, the specimens were placed again in the air-con- ditioned room for at least 27 days. In order to create a localized crack during the bending tests, a 20 mm deep notch was sawn in each beam at the bottom, in the middle of the specimens ( Fig. 2 ). 2.2. Experimental setup 2.2.1. Mechanical tests Mechanical tests were applied to create cracks, and thus, for the beams contain- ing embedded healing agent to trigger the healing mechanism. All beams were loaded by means of a crack width controlled three-point-bending test ( Fig. 3 ). An electro-mechanical testing device was used in order to reduce the amount of noise created by the equipment. The distance between the supports amounted 400 mm and the force was applied in the middle of the span. Between the supports and the beam a Teﬂon strip was provided in order to reduce the contact noise. The crack width was measured by means of a Crack Mouth Opening Displacement (CMOD) sensor. The CMOD was placed between two steel plates glued on each side of the notch on the bottom face of the concrete beam, as shown in Fig. 3. The crack width was increased with a velocity of 0.5 lm/s until a width of 300 lm was reached. When the ﬁnal crack width was reached, the beams were unloaded, giving cause to a decrease in crack width. The resulting crack width amounted to approximately 225 lm. One day after manual or autonomous crack repair, beams were reloaded using the same setup till an additional crack width of 300 lm was reached. Upon reloading, the crack healing efﬁciency was determined. 2.2.2. Acoustic emission measurements During crack creation as well as during reloading (see Section 2.2.1), specimens were instrumented with an AE system. When the concrete beams are loaded, elastic waves are emitted due to the occurrence of cracks. These waves are detected by AE sensors, attached to the surface of the beams. Analysis of the wave characteristics and the origin can provide useful information about the internal condition of the structure [22]. The AE instrumentation consisted of eight piezoelectric transducers with a nominal diameter of 25 mm and a relatively ﬂat frequency response below 1 MHz. The transducers were coupled to the concrete beams by means of hot glue on the positions shown in Fig. 3 and Table 3 . It is important that sensors are well distributed over the entire beam because only AE events which are recorded by at least four sensors can be accurately located [16]. The frequency range of the acquisition of wave forms was set from 1 kHz to 1 MHz. The slew rate trigger 1 was adjusted to 0.08 V/ ls and an input range of ±5 V was chosen. The detected signals were ampliﬁed with 54 dB gain ampliﬁers. Post-processing of the signals was con- trolled by the software package TransOcto AE, which was developed at the University of Stuttgart [25]. Before each test (crack creation and reloading), the pencil lead break test, also called Hsu–Nielsen test, was done for determining the reproducibility of the trans- ducer response [26]. The pencil lead break was produced at several ﬁxed positions indicated as P 1, P2 and P3 (see Fig. 3 and Table 3) on the surface of the concrete beams. The data obtained from the AE measurements were recorded with a different computer than the one which registered the parameters of the mechanical tests. In order to synchronize the data registered by both computers, a knock with a hammer was given onto each beam at the start (after a preload of 50 N h

equation system is over determined and has to be solved by iteration. As the event coordinates are unknown, the algorithm has to start with estimated values. When the sum of all error squares of the relative arrival times of all transducers becomes minimal, the best calculation for the event location is reached. This calculation is normally performed using a least square algorithm [18]. Localization of the events was done based on the ﬁltered signals and using the program Polar AE, which was developed at the University of Stuttgart [33]. Subsequently, a selection of the events was made based on their energy and the number of channels being able to clearly record the event. Events with a relative en- ergy higher than 1 and for which more than 5 channels could be used during local- ization were selected. All further analyses were performed on these selected events. Additionally, events were subdivided into different classes based onto their energy. Seven energy classes were made according to Table 5 . 2.4. Statistical analysis of the data For each of the experiments, an analysis of variance test (ANOVA-test) was applied to determine whether or not the means of various groups were equal. First, homogeneity of the variances was veriﬁed by means of a Levene’s test (signiﬁcance level 0.01). When equal variances were assumed, a Student–Newman–Keuls test (signiﬁcance level 0.05) was performed. For data with non-homogeneous variances, a Dunnett T3 test (signiﬁcance level 0.05) was used. 3. Results and discussion 3.1. Manual versus automatic onset time determination It was shown that the AE source locations were not only situ- ated at the tip of the crack but were distributed over a relatively wide area (see for example Fig. 4 ). This may be attributed to the development of the fracture process zone, which is not limited to the macro crack but also spreads around it [24]. It should be noticed, however, that the localization accuracy diminishes during crack growth. Due to the presence of the macro crack, signals can- not reach the sensors along the shortest path and will travel around the ﬁssure, which may lead to decrease in event localiza- tion accuracy [19]. In this research, deviations of the source coor- dinates varied from 0 to 100 mm. Besides, it is also noticed that several events were located on the side faces of the specimens, which was also observed by Kurz et al. [27]. Based on the large amount of events which needed to be ana- lyzed, automatic onset time detection was preferred. In order to be sure that automatic picking of the onset time does not inﬂuence the results, manual and automatic onset time determination were compared. In order to increase the accuracy of automatic onset time detection and thus localization, a selection was made of the events with a relative energy value higher than 1 and for which more than 5 channels were used in case automatic onset time determination was chosen. In this way, about 500 events remained from which we determined the onset time manually for comparison. In Fig. 4 , the located events are shown in the XY, XZ and YZ plane, for both manual and automatic onset time determination (specimen from ‘REF’ test series). The events located with the re- sults from the automatic Hinkley picker are not all in close proxim- ity to the manually picked events [27]. We are aware that automatic onset time determination is more precise when the Akaike Information Criterion (AIC) picker is used [27], however, this

The amount of strength regain, calculated according to Eq. (1),i s shown in Fig. 6. When considering the results shown in Fig. 6, a sig- niﬁcant difference can be noticed between the amount of strength regain obtained for untreated (‘REF’) and treated (‘EPO’, ‘PUR’, ‘SHC_PUR’ and ‘PURO’) cracks. While for the reference specimens the amount of strength regain is lower than 0%, the other test series show an average regain in strength varying from 35 till 80% of the original strength. Furthermore, a signiﬁcant difference is noticed be- tween the series ‘PUR’ and ‘PURO’ at the one hand and ‘EPO’ and ‘SHC_PUR’ at the other hand. It seems that manual healing of cracks by means of polyurethane is less effective than self-healing by using the same type of healing agent. As the same amount of strength re- gain is noticed for the test series ‘PUR’ and ‘PURO’, the lower efﬁciency of manual crack healing with polyurethane may not be attributed to the fact whether the crack is opened or not during re- pair. A possible explanation for the lower strength regain in case of manually healed cracks with polyurethane may be that both compo- nents of the healing agent are mixed before injection. Immediately after mixing of both compounds, the polyurethane starts foaming and maybe cannot penetrate deeply into the crack. Consequently, a lower regain in mechanical properties is obtained. In Fig. 6 , also the amount of stiffness regain, calculated accord- ing to Eq. (2), is shown. Again, a signiﬁcant difference is noticed between the stiffness regain obtained for untreated (‘REF’) and treated (‘EPO’, ‘PUR’, ‘SHC_PUR’ and ‘PURO’) cracks. For this param- eter, no signiﬁcant difference is noticed when polyurethane or epoxy is used to repair the cracks. Moreover, no signiﬁcant differ- ence is seen between manual and autonomous crack healing. How- ever, it should be noticed that the error bars, representing the standard error, are bigger so more caution is needed when making conclusions based on the amount of stiffness regain. 3.3. Self-healing efﬁciency proven by means of AE 3.3.1. Proof of crack healing It was proven that more acoustic emissions occurred upon reloading of specimens with repaired cracks. In Fig. 7 , a load–dis- placement curve is shown for one representative specimen of each test series together with the emitted events recorded during crack creation and reloading. Each event is represented by means of a dot and based on their energy class the dots are shown in different levels. Upon reloading of the beams, different phases could be distin- guished. During the ﬁrst phase, the crack just reopens in the case of reference specimens or the healing agent starts rupturing in the case of beams with healed cracks. When the crack opening which was reached during crack creation is reached again, new cracks starts to form in the concrete matrix. This is the start of the second phase. The third phase starts at the moment that the adjusted crack width is reached, loading is stopped and the crack width decreases. As the second and the third phase are almost equal for all test series, and quite some events are produced during the second phase, these events may mask the differences between the ﬁrst phases of the different test series. As we need to focus onto the ﬁrst phase (indicated with a rectangle in Fig. 7 ) a selected re- gion is magniﬁed at the right of each load–displacement curve in Fig. 7 . Events which were emitted upon reloading are represented by bigge

more events are noticed upon reloading of beams with healed cracks. However results need to be interpreted with care as for some test sample the ﬁrst phase only covers the elastic behavior of the beam while for other test series the ﬁrst phase also covers the post peak region. A signiﬁcant difference can be noted between the total cumula- tive energy released upon reloading of beams with cracks manually healed with epoxy and the other test series ( Fig. 8 ). For the speci- mens where cracks were healed with polyurethane (manually or automatically) no signiﬁcant difference was noted, compared to the reference specimens. However, from the amount of regain in mechanical properties it was seen that manual and autonomous crack healing with polyurethane is efﬁcient. be explained by the fact that crack formation into the polyurethane foam does not cause events with high energy. Although self healing of the cracks is not proven from the acoustic emission measure- ments, crack healing is proven in the case epoxy is used as healing agent. For AE to be used to indicate self-healing, healing agents such as epoxy should be used. However, epoxy was not used here for encapsulation as previous research showed that this type of polymer does not react under the imprecise mix ratio of the compounds obtained after release from the tubes [34]. In this re- search, epoxy was only used as reference for manual crack healing as this agent already showed its effectiveness for that purpose [15]. 3.3.2. Proof of tube breakage Breakage of the tubular capsules was proven from the AE mea- surements. It was found that only for specimens with self-healing properties (‘SHC_PUR’) events with an energy level of ‘7’ were noted ( Fig. 7 ). As these energetic events were accompanied by drops in load ( Fig. 7 ) and audible ‘pop’ sounds, they can deﬁnitely be attributed to breakage of the embedded tubes. In Fig. 9 , the cumulative emitted energy obtained during crack creation and reloading is shown for one representative specimen of each test series. It is noted that for specimens with embedded tubes the highest amounts of energy are released due to breakage of the tubes. Moreover, not only during crack creation but also dur- ing reloading a signiﬁcant higher amount of energy is released (Fig. 10). From this, it is conﬁrmed again that upon reloading addi- tional tubes break and thus additional healing agent may be re- leased. This means that also a second healing action may occur, which was also noticed from previous experiments [11]. Since events due to tube breakage can clearly be distinguished from other events, it is possible to localize only these, as it can be seen in Fig. 11 . As the residual between the hypocenter and the real location of the tube fracture planes remained below 5 mm, this counts as a proof of the localization accuracy. Although, the accuracy was tested earlier by means of pencil lead breaks onto the surface of the beams, an internal source is much better to test the sensor accuracy. The reason that not for all tubes an event is located in the vicinity, may be due to the dead time of AE record- ing. Once an AE signal is noticed, this signal is recorded during a preset time interval. During that time period other AE signals are not recorded. It can also be noticed that two events are located somewhat further from the tube fracture planes. This may be due to breakage of the tubes at the ends where they are anchored because of the applied sealant. 4. Conclusions From this study, it appears that ceramic tubes ﬁlled with poly- urethane can be used to heal concrete cracks autonomously. Auton- omous crack healing with polyurethane showed the same amount of strength and stiffness regain as manual crack healing with a high strength epoxy resin. Furthermore, it was shown that AE analysis is a very useful non-destructive testing technique to evaluate the crack healing efﬁciency in concrete. More AE events were noticed upon reloading of specimens with repaired cracks. However, the difference was only signiﬁcant when epoxy resin was used for 0 20000 40000 60000 80000 100000 120000 00 . 2 0 . 4 0 . 6 Series2 Series3 Series5 Series7 REF EPO PUR SHC_PUR Crack width [µm] 200 400 600 120,000 100,000 80,000 60,000 40,000 20,000

manual crack healing. Events due to breakage of the tubular capsules could clearly be distinguished from AE events caused by crack formation. High energetic events due to tube breakage were not only noticed during crack formation but also upon reloading. This counts as a proof that a second healing action may occur when the proposed self-healing mechanism is embedded.
